explanation.title: Writing Meaningful HTML with Semantic Elements
explanation.content:
# What is Semantic HTML? Semantic HTML means using elements that describe the **meaning** of their content, not just its visual appearance. Compare: ```html
...
...
...
...
...
...
``` Both render identically in a browser. But semantic HTML gives three major benefits: 1. **Accessibility** — screen readers use semantic elements to help blind users navigate: `
` means they can jump to the navigation, `
` means they can skip to the main content 2. **SEO** — search engines use `

`, `
`, `
` to understand what the page is about and rank it accordingly 3. **Maintainability** — other developers (and your future self) understand the structure immediately ## The Key Semantic Elements | Element | Meaning | Replaces | |---|---|---| | `
` | Page or section header | `
` | | `
` | Navigation links | `
` | | `
` | Primary page content (use once) | `
` | | `
` | Self-contained content (blog post, card) | `
` | | `
` | Thematic grouping of content | `
` | | `
` | Related but secondary content | `
` | | `
` | Page or section footer | `
` | | `

codeExample.explanation:
- `position: sticky; top: 0; z-index: 10` on `header` — the nav sticks to the top as the user scrolls
- `clamp(24px, 5vw, 44px)` on `h1` — fluid font size; scales between 24px and 44px with the viewport
- Anchor links like `href="#projects"` jump to the element with `id="projects"` on the same page
- `flex: 0 0 calc(33.333% - 14px)` — three-column cards with 20px gap compensation
- Mobile breakpoints convert three columns to two at 768px and one at 480px

exercise.title: Convert Divs to Semantic Elements
exercise.instructions:
The starter code uses div elements with id attributes for all structure. Replace each div with the appropriate semantic element: div#header becomes header, div#nav becomes nav, div#main becomes main, div#article becomes article, div#sidebar becomes aside, and div#footer becomes footer. Keep all existing CSS classes and styles unchanged. Only change the HTML tag names.

Expected: six semantic elements replacing six div elements.
exercise.starterCode:
My Site
HomeAbout

Welcome

Main content here.

Links

Related content.

© 2025 My Site
exercise.solutionCode:
My Site
HomeAbout

Welcome

Main content here.

Links

Related content.

© 2025 My Site
hints: 💡 Replace <div id="header"> with <header id="header"> — keep the id so the CSS still targets it.
💡 Replace <div id="nav"> with <nav id="nav">, div#sidebar with <aside id="sidebar">, div#article with <article id="article">.
💡 Replace the outermost div#main with <main id="main"> and div#footer with <footer id="footer">.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of using semantic HTML elements over generic divs?
options: Semantic elements load faster than divs
Semantic elements give meaning to content, helping screen readers and search engines
Semantic elements cannot be styled with CSS
Semantic elements automatically add visual styling
questions.explanation: Semantic elements communicate the role of their content to browsers, screen readers, and search engines. A nav element tells assistive technology 'this is the navigation', enabling keyboard shortcuts for blind users. Search engines use article and main to understand page content better. Divs convey no meaning — they are purely structural containers.

questions.question: How many main elements should a well-structured HTML page contain?
options: One per section
One per article
Exactly one — it represents the single primary content area
As many as needed for layout
questions.explanation: The main element should appear exactly once per page and contain the primary unique content of that page. Screen readers present main as a landmark that users can jump to directly. Having multiple main elements confuses both users and validators. Headers, footers, navigation, and sidebars all go outside main.

codeExample.explanation:
- `:root` CSS variables — a single place to change the entire colour scheme of the page
- `clamp(26px, 5vw, 48px)` on `h1` — fluid font size; scales between 26px and 48px with the viewport
- `display: flex; flex-direction: column; align-items: center` on `.hero` — stacks and centres the hero content
- `flex: 0 0 calc(33.333% - 14px)` — three-column card grid; the subtraction compensates for the `20px` gap
- `@media` blocks at the bottom — mobile-last is fine here since base styles are desktop; production code should be mobile-first

exercise.title: Add a Pricing Section to the Page
exercise.instructions: Add a pricing section to the landing page. Create a section element (class 'pricing') with an h2 heading 'Simple Pricing' and a flex row of two div elements (class 'plan'). Each plan needs an h3 name, a price paragraph, and two li items. Style .pricing with padding 60px 32px and max-width 800px centred. Style .plan with flex 1, white background, border-radius 10px, padding 24px, and border. Place the section inside main after the existing features section.

questions.question: Where should a link to an external CSS stylesheet be placed in an HTML document?
options: At the end of the body element
Inside the body, before any content
Inside the head element
After the closing html tag
questions.explanation: CSS link tags must go inside the head element so styles load before the browser renders any content. If you place them in the body, users briefly see unstyled content (a 'flash of unstyled content') before styles apply. JavaScript files, in contrast, are usually placed just before the closing body tag so they do not block HTML rendering.

questions.question: What does the loading="lazy" attribute on an img element do?
options: Makes the image load at low resolution first, then high resolution
Defers loading the image until it is close to entering the visible viewport
Prevents the image from loading at all until a button is clicked
Reduces the image file size automatically
questions.explanation: loading="lazy" tells the browser to defer downloading an image until the user scrolls near it. Images below the fold (not immediately visible) are not fetched on page load, reducing initial page weight and speeding up the time-to-interactive. Images above the fold (in the first screenful) should use loading="eager" or omit the attribute entirely.

explanation.title: Putting It All Together: Build Your Personal Portfolio
explanation.content:
# The Mini Project

You have now learned every fundamental skill in HTML and CSS:

1. **HTML structure** — DOCTYPE, head, body, metadata
2. **Text elements** — headings, paragraphs, lists, inline tags
3. **Links and images** — anchors, img, tables, forms
4. **CSS selectors** — element, class, ID, pseudo-class, descendant
5. **Box model** — margin, padding, border, box-sizing
6. **Typography and colour** — font stacks, colours, CSS variables
7. **Flexbox** — axes, alignment, flex-wrap, flex-grow
8. **Responsive design** — media queries, mobile-first, fluid units
9. **Semantic HTML** — header, nav, main, article, section, footer

In this lesson, you will build a **personal portfolio website** — the most useful project a beginner can make. Employers and clients look at portfolios before deciding whether to hire someone.

## Portfolio Structure

A minimal portfolio has four sections:

```
header  ← your name + nav links
hero    ← your headline + one-sentence description
projects ← 3-4 cards, each with a title, description, and link
contact ← email link or a form
footer  ← copyright + social links
```

## What Makes a Good Portfolio?

- **One clear h1** — your name or professional title
- **A specific description** — not "I am a developer" but "I build Python and JavaScript web apps"
- **Real project links** — even small projects count
- **Clean, readable design** — white space is your friend
- **Mobile responsive** — recruiters check on phones

## Deploying Your Portfolio

GitHub Pages is the fastest free hosting for HTML/CSS sites:

1. Create a repository named `yourusername.github.io`
2. Push your `index.html` and `style.css` files
3. Your site is live at `https://yourusername.github.io`

Netlify and Vercel are also free and require no Git knowledge — drag and drop your folder.

In the code example below, you will see a complete, fully styled, responsive portfolio page using every concept from this course. Study the structure carefully — your exercise is to personalise and extend it.

codeExample.explanation:
- `position: sticky; top: 0; z-index: 10` on `header` — the nav sticks to the top while the user scrolls through the page
- `clamp(24px, 5vw, 44px)` on `h1` — the name scales fluidly between phone and desktop sizes
- Anchor links like `href="#projects"` — jump to the element with `id="projects"` on the same page
- `flex: 0 0 calc(33.333% - 14px)` for project cards — three columns with 20px gap compensation
- Mobile breakpoints convert the three-column grid to two columns at 768px and one column at 480px

exercise.title: Personalise the Portfolio
exercise.instructions:
Take the portfolio starter and personalise it: change the name in header strong and h1 to your name, update the role paragraph to your actual role or aspiration, replace the four skill span tags with your real skills, update the three project cards with your own project names and descriptions, and update the contact email href to your address. Keep all HTML structure and CSS unchanged.

Expected: your name visible, four skill tags, three project cards.

questions.question: What is the fastest free way to host a personal HTML/CSS website publicly?
options: Buying a server and installing Apache
Emailing the HTML file to visitors
Using GitHub Pages, Netlify, or Vercel — all support static HTML for free
Uploading to Google Drive and sharing the link
questions.explanation: GitHub Pages, Netlify, and Vercel all offer free hosting for static websites (pure HTML, CSS, and JavaScript). GitHub Pages is set up by creating a repository named yourusername.github.io. Netlify and Vercel support drag-and-drop deployment — no terminal required. All three provide HTTPS automatically.

questions.question: Which of the following is the most important thing to include on a developer portfolio?
options: A photo of your workspace
Animated transitions on every element
Actual links to real projects you have built
A long list of every technology you have heard of
questions.explanation: Employers and clients hire based on what you can build, not what you claim. Links to real projects — even small ones — are far more convincing than a list of skills. A project link proves ability; a skill tag only makes a claim. Start with one or two genuine projects rather than an impressive-looking but empty portfolio.

questions.question: What do the six concepts HTML, CSS selectors, box model, Flexbox, media queries, and semantic HTML have in common?
options: They are all optional features only used by advanced developers
They are the complete foundation needed to build any static webpage
They only apply to mobile development
They must be learned in a different order than listed
questions.explanation: These six areas form the complete foundation of static web development. HTML provides structure, CSS selectors target elements, the box model controls spacing and sizing, Flexbox handles layout, media queries add responsiveness, and semantic HTML adds meaning and accessibility. With these six skills you can build any website that does not require a server or database.
